speaker: interviewer
text: --the 15th, 1995. Interview with Sol Urbach. Tape 2. You were talking about the ghetto. And how did you get into the ghetto? You went in later, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the detail of that moment on how we entered the ghetto is very sketchy in my mind. But, but whatever I, I remember was that we knew somebody that would help us walk through the gate. We needed to walk through a gate which was actually guarded by a German. They, they, they were German guards already. But if I recall, there was a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. That is, the Jewish policemen were still active and still had-- and played a role. And one of those would lead us into the ghetto. It was not a very easy-- it was a risky situation at that time because we were supposed to follow the order and enter the ghetto earlier on. But somehow the family thought that they could stay out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was an extremely [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] crowded quarters. There were people all over. There were people in buildings. There were people outside of buildings, people milling around, people not knowing what to do. The rumor mills, the talks, the conversations [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that at times, as a group of people, we didn't even notice that, that we were in deep trouble. We simply thrived on the idea that we are, we no longer faced the anti-Semites on the outside, and we were amongst Jews. And, and a life began to flourish inside the ghetto. In other words, there were people repairing shoes, and there were people listening to music, and they were almost a, a-- some semblance of normal life took on, took hold. And, and for a while, everybody was satisfied to be there. This was not the worst of places to be, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, there were schools. I attended not a formal kind of a school, but I had opportunity to attend some classes-- a religious school more so than anything else. So we had a chance to do that. But there were no schools, no public schools as we knew them before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as we stayed in the ghetto, the situation got worse every day. And, and the reason for that was that the Germans would come into the ghetto and occasionally round up 100 people, 500 people and take them into trucks and take them outside of the ghetto to work someplace for the Germans. And these work squadrons that they picked up in the ghetto would sometimes go out to shovel snow in the city of Cracow, dig ditches. And in those days, they were preparing for the air raids all the time. So the Jews would be digging trenches on the outside of the ghetto in the area of the parks and so on, unloading carloads of merchandise for the Germans. In many situations, those Jews were returned to the ghetto after a day's work. But on many occasions, some of the Jews did not return to the ghetto. In other words, whatever happened to them on the outside we don't know. Some people simply disappeared, were killed on the outside of the ghetto. So this began to instill fear into the population of the ghetto that if you get taken out from the ghetto, you don't know what's going to happen to us, whether we will ever return. In many situations they rounded up people to, to, in, in what the Germans called Aktions. And in those Aktions, they would take people, round them up, and bring them to, to some concentration camps, in many cases to Auschwitz or other similar camps, work, work camps where they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time, did you know where they were taking those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They simply disappeared. At the time, we would not know what was happening to the people. Rumors would have a lot of things, you know, that they were killed, that they were taken away, but we did not know any facts. We did not have any idea, at least I didn't. If there were some people in the ghetto-- there is a good chance that there were-- that knew a little more than I did as a youngster. And so this, this began to play a role in our psyche in living in the ghetto that we, we wouldn't know what the future holds for us. At that time, we, all of, all the eight of us were living in the, in the, basement room in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- sometime in 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I happened to be in the street of the ghetto, in [NON-ENGLISH] when two small trucks with Germans came into the ghetto to round up some people for work. Of course, we didn't know where, where those, where those people would be taken at that time because nobody had to bother explaining to you why they're loading you into a truck. They simply loaded-- at that time it appeared that they loaded 100 of us into those two small pickups. And they were covered trucks. I remember them. The, the SS guards appeared like spic and span, clean, quickly giving orders, shouting orders for, for those people they rounded up, they loaded on the trucks. And they took us out of the ghetto. Of course, the, the people that they took out of the ghetto were full of fear. They had no idea where they were being taken. But as it happens, they took the 100 of us to a back yard of a factory in the city of Cracow. In other words, they crossed the, crossed into the city of Cracow itself, and they dropped us off in the back yard of a factory. We had no idea what this factory is, who, who runs this factory. We had absolutely no idea why we are there. We had an idea maybe we'll go to work by then. But when we came there, the SS guards quickly kicked us into formation. And as we stood in an orderly assembly, the SS guards shouted out to a civilian that was there to inspect the people they brought in for work. As it turns out to be this civilian was Oskar Schindler. And in 1942 when he came over to inspect us, he was looking to receive 100 people that would be strong workers. When he took a look at this group of 100 people, he has selected two people out of this group and one of them myself. And he yelled out to the SS guards in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What that means is that I don't need any children here. We have to make some allowances to Schindler for that time. This is 1942--

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was sometime the end of summer. And in, in 1942, Schindler was neither an important person that the SS guards would recognize. And Schindler potentially was looking for strong workers at the same time. And he maybe was also naive somewhat, not knowing that if he points out children that the SS guards, in fact, could do away with the children very easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all this has played a role, but the best part is that the SS guards did not recognize Oskar Schindler as an important individual. And they yelled back to him to keep what they delivered. And at that, the SS guards left, and we stayed to, at the Oskar Schindler enamel works factory, as it was known at that time, Emalia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed as workers, as slave laborers. I don't know of any other way-- there were no compensations for the workers, for us. We were simply ordered to stay there and work, and we worked. And what is meant from now on is that I, together with the group of the 100, would be leaving the ghetto for a shift, whether it was a day shift or a night shift. We would leave from the ghetto under the escorts of the German SS guards. We would leave the ghetto and march daily to the factory of Oskar Schindler and work in that factory. Food was in much better supply. Somehow Schindler had some food on the premises in the factory. So we were fed somewhat better than I would have been fed in the ghetto itself. But my whole family of, of three brothers, two sisters, parents, remained in the ghetto, and I would walk out daily. My brother and sister and father were occasionally also rounded up and taken out of the ghetto and delivered back to the ghetto. But they were rounded up for work and, and would be returned. After either the same day or the next day, they would be returned to the ghetto. And I continued working at Oskar Schindler's factory. Now, this work continued until March 12, 1943. We would be marching out daily from the ghetto. And on March 12, we-- I walked out of the ghetto, saying goodbye to my family as I normally would when I left for work. But on March 12 when we got to Oskar Schindler's factory, Oskar Schindler announced to the group that we cannot go back to the ghetto, that the ghetto is being liquidated, that there is an Aktion in the ghetto itself, meaning that people are being killed there. There is-- there are brutalities going on. People are taken away from there. And we would be better off staying in his factory than going back to the ghetto. There is a good chance that at that time that he had the option. He had the option of delivering us to the ghetto and therefore delivering us to be exposed to the, all the brutalities that were going on. Or he had the chance to make a quick decision that he's going to keep us over. And he made the decision to keep us over. And while we were staying there for the next three or four nights at the factory, sleeping on the, on the floor or wherever we could find a place in the factory, as we know now-- at that time we didn't know that-- but in fact, the Germans had decided to liquidate the Cracow ghetto. They entered with extreme, extremely well-armed squadrons of, of SS. And they began to kill and take out people to be shipped to other concentration camps. This was, indeed, the last time that I saw my family. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After the liquidation of the ghetto, after the, the ghetto population was silenced, those that were killed were lying in the streets of the ghetto. The streets of the ghetto looked like an outdoor morgue. And we know that-- also I was not at the time in the ghetto, but I know that because after the three, four days, Schindler was ordered to deliver the 100 of us to the concentration camp of Krakau-Plaszow, where not only the 100 of us were shipped, but those remnants that survived the liquidation of the Cracow ghetto were also shipped to the same concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those remnants would tell us on what took place in the ghetto. And, at the same time, when we arrived there, we could, we could see the dump trucks of bodies arriving [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with bodies that they cleaned up from the ghetto itself. They were simply cleaning up the ghetto and throwing the bodies on those dump trucks and bringing them into the Krakau-Plaszow concentration camp where there were open pits prepared for them. And they were dumping these bodies into the, into those pits.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that not only myself but many people that were in my group would, at that time, think that possibly our families are arriving in that manner, that they were, in fact, possibly killed in the ghetto, and they are being dumped into the open pits. One of the people that we knew, a friend of mine and my older brother's, also told me a story that he witnessed in the ghetto itself during the liquidation, that my brother was on one side of the street when he noticed that the rest of the family is on the other side of the street. There is a good chance that he was selected out to go on to another concentration camp because he was young and looked very strong. There is a good chance that he was selected to, to remain alive. But when he noticed his family on the other side of the street, he made a dash to join them. And on crossing the street, he was shot down in the middle of the street. So there was an eyewitness telling me the story that my older brother was, in fact, killed in the ghetto itself. But the rest of the family-- the fate of the rest of the family was totally unknown to me. And I began life inside of a concentration camp. And this is 1943. I'm 17 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Never been away far from my family, never knowing how to get along on my own, I found myself in the, in a concentration camp where I practically didn't know anyone. And even if I did, they were lost in that concentration camp because there were thousands upon thousands of people. I don't have a good idea of how many thousands of people there were at that particular time, but if I were to guess at it, there were well over 20,000 people in the ghetto, in the concentration camp at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became an inmate of this concentration camp. Life was unbearable. Constant fear. Food in short supply. All alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Looking undernourished and not in the best of health, because the war was on for three years already. So I'm not really equipped to deal with the heavy burden of a concentration camp. But there I was. In fact, I was assigned to work in a stone quarry, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which actually improved my life, because in the stone quarry, while it was extremely hard work, there was a little better supply of food for those that worked in the stone quarry. I remember one incident where I was in the barracks of the concentration camp, and it was a huge barrack-- I don't know how many people would been there, but it could've been 1,000 people, people sleeping on three-high berth. I was on the top one. And people would usually sleep in a manner that they would be with their heads towards the center of the room rather than towards the wall because they always wanted to hear and see what was going on. If they had to react to something, they would, they, they would be able to do that. And I remember being in one of those-- sleeping on that high berth one day when an SS man came into the barrack and yelled out, Urbach! [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, as I described before, the fear was constant. It was always there. The, the, the, necessity to respond and listen to the German orders was always there. So when the SS guard inside the barrack yelled out Urbach, another fellow stuck out his head, responded to the, to the call of Urbach. And I, not being sure whether he was calling me or my name or some other similar name, I also stuck out my head. So now the SS guard was confused on who he really is supposed to be picking up. So he took the two of us. And he took us down to the SS guards' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quarters. This was not the headquarters where Amon Goth would've been, but this was where the guards would have been. And if I remember, the name of the guard there was Hujar. And he was in charge. When we arrived, he got a hold of this fellow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other fellow that came with me was by the name of Orbach, with an O-- spelled with an O. And there is a good chance that they wanted him and not me. But we will never know that. But as soon as we arrived there at this quarter of the guards-- these were mostly volunteers. These were not Germans, but volunteer guards-- Lithuanians, Poles, possibly. And as soon as we arrived there, they took this fellow Orbach into the second room and began to torture him. And I was sitting in this room observing what was going on in the other room where he was being tortured. He was beaten. He was-- when he was unconscious they dumped water on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to see this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was able to see. I was not able to hear. But I was able to see because there was a glass door. I was able to see that. I saw even the man being hung by the, with his hands tied to the back. He was hung on those hands. And when he was turning blue or he was going unconscious, they would lower those ropes and he would fall to the floor. They would dump water on him again, and the torture would continue. It was only after some-- you know, if I had to judge time, it seemed like hours, but most likely it was minutes that they-- he was tortured. And after a short while, somebody came running in. One of the SS guards came running in telling the others something. And at that, they released this fellow Orbach together with me, and we went back to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was never tortured as this fellow Orbach was. He was tortured, but he was still alive and in good shape, good enough shape that we could walk back to our barracks. And we survived that. Did you ever find out why they took you in the first place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumors only. And the rumor would have it that somebody spoke of this fellow, Orbach, as a Red Orbach. Now, he had red hair, and so they spoke of him as Red Orbach, meaning a communist. And that supposedly was the reason for that visit and for him being tortured. But whether this was the truth or not, I don't know. I wouldn't-- I did not know it at that time. And I still don't know whether this is the absolute truth. But he was a redhead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so it took a, a while. It took maybe a, a few weeks, where we remained-- this 100, this group of 100 remained inmates of Krakau-Plaszow without knowing whether Schindler is still around, whether Schindler is, is in our, in our future or not. But lo and behold, Schindler was busy out there working to get the 100 of us out from this, from this concentration camp to come back to work for him. And after a few weeks of work and stay in Krakau, in the Krakau-Plaszow concentration camp, the 100 of us began marching out from the concentration camp to work at the enamel work factory, at the Emalia, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life became, life began to look up a little bit again. We were working outside of the camp and returning in the evening to the, to the Krakau-Plaszow camp. We were still exposed to the extreme brutalities. And, at times, on many occasions, we would stand in the gathering plaza in the Appellplatz, where we would probably be together there every morning or every evening for counting purposes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in those, in those gatherings on the, on the Appellplatz, Amon Goth with his cohorts would come out and walk amongst the people. The inmates, the inmates were-- at this point, we would be froze still, because we-- as soon as Amon Goth would leave his villa, rumor took off, and it filtered right through the ranks of the inmates. And we all stood so still that you could hear a pin drop, even though there were 20,000 people or so standing there. Because of fear, we were absolutely silent, almost afraid to breathe, because he would-- Amon Goth would have a habit of walking by these lines of people and shooting people that they didn't like or lynch people that they-- for one or another reason, as they'd walk by they would do it. And lynchings were going on at the same time. And so we were exposed to these things. But the good news was that in the mornings or in the evenings we would leave that camp and go to work for Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually witness those shootings or lynchings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Oh, yes. We would also hear-- in addition to, to witnessing these shootings and lynchings, we would also hear the many shootings that were going on far away from us. There was a hill not far from where we stood. And on that hill, they were killing people on a constant basis, practically. So we would hear these shots ring out. We would also witness hangings. Hangings were going on. The movie does not show any hangings. The movie does not show hangings. But there were hangings going on. When those hangings were going on, all the inmates had to file by and, and look closely as to what hung people looked like. I remember some of my friends-- two, two brothers-- that were hung, brothers by the name of Spielman. I actually went to cheder together with them. They were hung. Supposedly, rumor had it that they were trying to escape. But, of course, all these things might have not been true at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we would continue our work at Oskar Schindler's factory until sometime in 1943. Toward the end of 1943, to the best of my knowledge, an order came out, the Jews will no longer be able to work outside of a concentration camp. So, therefore, we were stopped from going to Oskar Schindler's work. We had to work in the camp again. And, once again, I became a full inmate of the, of the camp. I was staying there and working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but unbeknown to me at that time again, Schindler, Schindler was told about it that he can no longer have people walking out of the concentration camp to work for him. And he would have to give up his workforce. But Schindler, at that time, after witnessing the liquidation of the ghetto as we know now from readings and the movie-- we know that after Schindler has witnessed the brutalities of his fellow Germans that they were committing against the Jews, he was no longer a loyal German. And he began to scheme his own future, his future. And that would mean that he, if he-- evidently, he felt that if he could save some Jews from destruction, he would also be able to save himself. And he concocted a plan that his industry-- so vital for the Germans-- that maybe he could build a concentration camp of his own adjoining his factory of Emalia in Cracow.
